summary: High-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance include: European Union now requires compliance with Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 for microbiology limits. GCC has new labelling requirements for general labelling, Halal food, and mandatory expiration dates. India now mandates allergen declarations. Japan has introduced several critical rules: only MHLW-specified safe additives are allowed; food with contamination, foreign substances, or causing harm is prohibited; specific animal products with diseases are not for sale; agricultural chemical/drug residues must not exceed MHLW limits; toxic substances are generally prohibited; mandatory notification for harm from designated ingredients; and food contaminated with pathogens is not allowed if harmful. Singapore prohibits non-permitted food additives and contaminants exceeding SFA limits, including specific residues (Diethylstibestrol, Hexoestrol, Dienoestrol); ingredients and additives must now be declared *in the list of ingredients* on food labels. Taiwan requires new allergen labeling for specific ingredients (Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame, milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish) if present, and for sulphite if ≥10 mg/kg. Thailand has prohibited certain food products from production, import, or sale. The United States has prohibited specific substances and delisted color additives. Major food allergens (including sesame as of Jan 2023) require declaration, with new contaminant guidance levels for 3-MCPD (1 ppm) and Aflatoxins (20 ppb), and general chemical contaminant levels requiring verification. Labelling changes include mandatory allergen preventive controls, declaration of Cochineal Extract/Carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, a defined standard for 'Gluten-Free' claims, specific mandatory major food allergen declaration formats, and sulfiting agents declaration if ≥10 ppm.

changes.parameter: Food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine) and process hygiene criteria
changes.newLimit: Compliance with Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 required
changes.notes: This regulation defines the acceptability of a product or a batch of food and lays down process hygiene criteria to indicate the correct functioning of the production process.
This regulation sets out the general requirements for labelling of prepackaged food.
This regulation outlines the general requirements for Halal food.
This regulation specifies mandatory expiration dates for food products.

Specific requirements for allergen declaration on food labels for all food products, including seasoning and snack products, are detailed in the referenced compendium. Manufacturers must consult the full document for compliance.

Additives (excluding natural flavoring agents and generally consumed substances used as additives) and food containing them are prohibited for sale, production, import, etc., unless the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies them as unlikely to cause harm to human health.

changes.parameter: Contamination, foreign substances, or other grounds causing harm
changes.notes: General prohibition for food likely to cause harm to human health.

Prohibition of selling meat, bones, milk, organs, and blood from livestock or poultry that have or are suspected to have specified diseases or abnormalities, or have died, except for cases specified by Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare Order.

changes.parameter: Residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, veterinary drugs
changes.newLimit: Must not exceed MHLW-specified safe quantities
changes.notes: Food containing residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, or pharmaceutical products for animals in quantities exceeding MHLW-specified safe limits is prohibited. Verification of specific MHLW limits is required.

changes.newLimit: Not allowed (unless specified safe by MHLW)
changes.notes: Prohibition of food or additives containing or suspected to contain toxic or harmful substances, unless the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies them as unlikely to cause harm.

changes.parameter: Notification of harm from designated ingredients
changes.notes: Businesses handling food with MHLW-designated ingredients must notify the relevant prefectural governor, etc. without delay if the food causes or may cause harm to human health.

changes.newLimit: Not allowed if likely to cause harm
changes.notes: Prohibition of food contaminated with pathogens or suspected to be so, if likely to cause harm to human health.
An application can be submitted to SFA for evaluation of non-permitted additives.

changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
changes.notes: All food businesses must comply.
Prohibition under Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations.

changes.oldLimit: Must be declared on food labels
changes.newLimit: Must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels
changes.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.newLimit: Presence as an ingredient or food additive
changes.notes: Labeling is required if these are present as ingredients or food additives.
Labeling is required if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 mg/kg or more.
Certain food products are prohibited from production, import, or sale in Thailand.

changes.parameter: Prohibited Substances and Delisted Color Additives
changes.notes: Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) and delisted color additives in 21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30.

The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. All FDA requirements applicable to major food allergens, including labeling and manufacturing requirements, apply to sesame as of January 1, 2023.
Guidance Level for 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
Action Level for total aflatoxins in foods, peanuts and peanut products, pistachio nuts, and Brazil nuts.

changes.parameter: Chemical Contaminants (Tolerances, Action Levels, Guidance Levels)
changes.notes: Contaminant levels for poisonous or deleterious substances are published in various guidance documents and regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509). The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list.

The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens. Facilities must put written procedures in place.
Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.
The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the 'gluten-free' claim were established on August 12, 2020.

The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') OR immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.'). The specific type of tree nut, species of fish, and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives. Specific provisions are available in the searchable online database.

changes.parameter: Maximum levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants
changes.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) and guideline levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants in food. These are detailed in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and other related texts.

changes.parameter: Contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)

changes.notes: This standard outlines general requirements for data labels on packaged products, applicable to seasoning, snack food, and processed foods.

This regulation outlines general labelling requirements for all prepackaged foodstuffs, which would include allergen declarations, ingredient lists, and potentially warnings relevant to seasoning, snack food, and processed foods.
This regulation specifies mandatory shelf life periods for food products, which must be declared on the label for seasoning, snack food, and processed foods.
This regulation provides guidelines for optional shelf life periods for food products, which may be declared on the label for seasoning, snack food, and processed foods.
This standard relates to the accurate declaration of product quantity on labels for prepackaged products, including seasoning, snack food, and processed foods.

General labelling and display requirements for food products, including seasoning and snack products, are detailed in the referenced compendium. This includes aspects like nutritional information, ingredient list, net quantity, etc. Manufacturers must consult the full document for compliance.

changes.parameter: Declaration of salt/sodium content and related claims

Manufacturers must consult the full document for compliance.

This circular letter specifies provisions for the production, import, registration, and circulation of Dinitrogen Monoxide (N2O) as a food additive. Actual limits and conditions must be verified from the full document.

This regulation defines food categories, which is essential for understanding the scope of other food regulations, proper product classification, and related labelling requirements. The full document must be consulted.
Specific regulations for processed foods, including seasoning and snack products, must be consulted in the BPOM legal database. There are 281 regulations listed under 'Pangan Olahan'.

Imported livestock/poultry meat, milk, organs, and products thereof must have certificates from the exporting country's governmental organization, confirming they are free from specified diseases/abnormalities.

Food and additives specified by MHLW as requiring certification of production site sanitation management must not be imported for sale unless accompanied by certificates from the exporting country's governmental organization.

changes.parameter: Production, processing, use, cooking, preservation methods, and ingredients
changes.notes: Food or additives must conform to criteria established by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare for methods of production, processing, use, cooking, or preservation, and to standards for ingredients. Verification of specific MHLW criteria and standards is required.

Food and additives specified by MHLW as requiring important process control measures must only be imported if produced or processed in MHLW-specified countries, regions, or facilities that implement those measures.
